content:
# Revelation Chapter 12 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE, page 286 | **Source Image:** REV_012_page_001.jpg

1. And a great sign was seen in the heaven: a woman having been clothed with the sun, and the moon under her feet, and upon her head a crown of twelve stars,

2. and having [a child] in womb, and she cries out, being in labor-pain and being tormented to give birth.

3. And another sign was seen in the heaven; and behold, a great fiery-red dragon, having seven heads and ten horns, and upon his heads seven diadems,

4. and his tail drags the third of the stars of the heaven and cast them to the earth. And the dragon stood before the woman who was about to give birth, so that when she gives birth he might devour her child.

5. And she bore a son, a male, who is about to shepherd all the nations with a rod of iron; and her child was snatched up to God and to his throne.

6. And the woman fled into the wilderness, where she has there a place having been prepared by God, that there they might nourish her a thousand two hundred sixty days.

7. And there was war in the heaven: Michael and his angels [went] to make war with the dragon. And the dragon made war, and his angels,

8. and he did not prevail, nor was a place found for them any longer in the heaven.

9. And the great dragon was cast [out], the ancient serpent, the one called Devil and Satan, the one deceiving the whole inhabited world — he was cast to the earth, and his angels were cast with him.

10. And I heard a great voice in the heaven saying, "Now has come the salvation and the power and the kingdom of our God and the authority of his Christos, because the accuser of our brothers was cast [down], the one accusing them before our God day and night.

11. And they overcame him through the blood of the Lamb and through the word of their testimony, and they did not love their life unto death.

12. Because of this, rejoice, [O] heavens and those tabernacling in them! Woe to the earth and the sea, because the devil has come down to you having great wrath, knowing that he has [only] a short time."

13. And when the dragon saw that he was cast to the earth, he pursued the woman who bore the male [child].

14. And there were given to the woman the two wings of the great eagle, so that she might fly into the wilderness to her place, where she is nourished there a time and times and half a time, away from the face of the serpent.

15. And the serpent cast out of his mouth after the woman water as a river, so that he might make her carried-away-by-the-river.

16. And the earth helped the woman, and the earth opened its mouth and swallowed the river which the dragon cast out of his mouth.

17. And the dragon was wrathful at the woman, and went away to make war with the rest of her seed, those keeping the commandments of God and having the testimony of Yeshua.

18. And he stood upon the sand of the sea.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "having been clothed with the sun" (peribeblēmenē ton hēlion):** Perfect passive participle — the woman is wrapped in the sun as a garment. The imagery echoes Song of Songs 6:10 and possibly Genesis 37:9 (Joseph's dream of sun, moon, and stars).

2. **v.3 — "fiery-red" (pyrros):** Sinaiticus reads pyrros — flame-colored, blood-red. The same adjective describes the second horse in 6:4. The dragon's color signals violence and bloodshed.

3. **v.4 — "drags" (syrei):** Present tense — the dragon's tail is actively sweeping stars down. The verb syro means to drag or trail along the ground.

4. **v.5 — "a son, a male" (huion arsen):** The double identification is emphatic and grammatically unusual — arsen is neuter, modifying the masculine huion. Sinaiticus preserves this construction. The phrase echoes Isaiah 66:7 (LXX). The destiny "to shepherd all nations with a rod of iron" quotes Psalm 2:9.

5. **v.6 — "a thousand two hundred sixty days":** The same period as the forty-two months of 11:2 and the time of the two witnesses in 11:3. Sinaiticus reads the number clearly.

6. **v.7 — "Michael and his angels":** Sinaiticus reads ho Michaēl kai hoi angeloi autou. The article ho before Michael is notable — "the Michael," treating the name as a title.

7. **v.9 — Fourfold identification of the dragon:** Sinaiticus preserves the full chain: ho drakōn ho megas, ho ophis ho archaios, ho kaloumenos Diabolos kai ho Satanas — "the great dragon, the ancient serpent, the one called Devil and Satan." Four names for one entity, linking Genesis 3 (the serpent) to the cosmic adversary.

8. **v.11 — "they did not love their life unto death":** ouk ēgapēsan tēn psychēn autōn achri thanatou. The aorist ēgapēsan describes their decisive choice. This echoes the words of Yeshua in John 12:25.

9. **v.14 — "a time and times and half a time":** kairon kai kairous kai hēmisy kairou — directly from Daniel 7:25, 12:7 (LXX). This equals 3.5 years = 42 months = 1,260 days, the same period expressed three different ways in Revelation.

10. **v.15 — "carried-away-by-the-river" (potamophorēton):** A rare compound adjective — potamos (river) + phorētos (carried). Sinaiticus preserves this unusual word. The flood-weapon against the woman may echo Pharaoh's pursuit at the Red Sea.

11. **v.17 — "the testimony of Yeshua":** tēn marturian Iēsou — the genitive Iēsou may be subjective (the testimony that Yeshua gave) or objective (testimony about Yeshua). The ambiguity is preserved in translation.

12. **v.18 — "he stood" (estathē):** Sinaiticus reads estathē — aorist passive, "he was made to stand" or "he stood." Some manuscripts read estathēn ("I stood"), making this the beginning of chapter 13. Sinaiticus clearly attributes the standing to the dragon, not to John.

— EMET
